step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("diabetes ophthalmology screening timeline")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

After reviewing the retrieved evidence, I notice conflicting information regarding the timing of ophthalmic screening in diabetes. While some sources indicate that type 1 diabetes patients should begin screening five years after diagnosis, type 2 diabetes patients should have screening *at* diagnosis and annually afterward. For instance, source [11] explicitly says: "patients with diabetes mellitus type 2 should have fundus evaluation at the time of diagnosis and annually thereafter." Source [16] notes that approximately 10% of type 2 patients already show retinal changes at diagnosis. Given that the question involves a patient with one year of type 2 diabetes and no complications, the strongest guidance points to initiating screening at diagnosis—immediately—or at the earliest possible moment. Although some older references mention waiting until five years, modern guidelines prioritize early detection due to the high rate of existing retinal changes at diagnosis and the ability to intervene preemptively. Therefore, I agree with the original position that screening should happen as early as feasible.
\boxed{\text{A}}
